content_of_post_to_comment:
One pattern I’ve noticed in my own life is that I’ll get motivated, work hard for a week or two, not see any meaningful results, get frustrated, and eventually quit. Then a few weeks or months later I’ll get inspired again, start over, and repeat the same cycle.

I think a lot of us are told that hard work pays off, but we’re rarely told what it feels like in the months or years before it pays off. Most goals involve doing the same boring things over and over again with little visible progress.

Whether it’s fitness, learning a skill, building a business, studying, getting a better job, improving your finances, or anything else, I’m curious:

What kept you going when there was no evidence that your efforts were working?

How did you deal with the monotony and repetition?

Did you focus on the process, distract yourself somehow, track progress obsessively, or just rely on discipline?

Were there periods where you genuinely thought it wasn’t going to work?

And finally, what was the moment when you realized all that effort had actually been worth it?

I’m interested in hearing real stories, especially from people
whose success took years rather than weeks or months.

Looking back, what do you think separated the people who eventually made it from those who gave up too early?
comment: I’ve noticed the hardest phase is when the old results disappear but the new identity hasn’t formed yet. A lot of people quit because they measure daily outcomes instead of whether they’re becoming the kind of person who can produce those outcomes. Sometimes the only proof you have is that you’re still showing up months later.
